publications.title: T cell receptors employ diverse strategies to target a p53 cancer neoantigen.
publications.authors: Wu D, Gowathaman R, Pierce BG, Mariuzza RA.
publications.executive_summary: The study examined four oligoclonal T cell receptors (38-10, 12-6, 1a2, and 6-11) that recognize a p53R175H neoantigen presented by HLA-A2, a driver mutation in the p53 oncogene frequently found in human cancers. While three of these TCRs achieve neoantigen selectivity through direct contacts with the R175H mutation itself, TCR 6-11 employs a distinct indirect mechanism: it discriminates mutant from wild-type p53 primarily by exploiting differences in solvation energetics around position 175, achieving 60-fold selectivity without direct mutation contacts. This work demonstrates that T cells recognize the same cancer neoantigen through mechanistically diverse strategies, a finding that has important implications for adoptive cell therapy design and understanding how the immune system can generate tumor-specific responses with sufficient selectivity to kill malignant cells while sparing normal tissue expressing wild-type p53.
